narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school part-time, your profile shows determination to balance studies with other commitments, which is less common since only about 7% of students opt for part-time status. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 56% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on how your key qualifications stack up against patterns seen in students who typically pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score of 20, which is notably below the average of around 37 and signaled to the model a higher risk compared to stronger scorers who usually succeed. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since your 3.10 is close to the typical 3.21 average and reflects steady performance. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st—far below the average 6th decile—had a moderate pushing effect toward failure, suggesting an early academic struggle that the model associates with bar exam challenges. Overall, your lower LSAT and early decile outweighed the positive from your GPA, placing you outside the profile of students who generally pass, like those with higher test scores and consistent rankings. While your lower-middle family income bracket and part-time plans played smaller roles, they didn't shift the balance enough. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, as that's the biggest lever here, and seek advising on strengthening early academic showings through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplication. Strengthening these could realign your profile with successful passers, opening doors at reconsideration or other programs. You're close in some areas, so focused improvements can make a real difference next time.
